Bayern's ultras group, Schickeria Munchen, runs an ongoing campaign honoring Kurt Landauer, the club's Jewish president before World War II who was persecuted by the Nazis. The club itself has publicly acknowledged this campaign, not just outside journalists. Because it's a live, ongoing project rather than something from the past, this is designated current.

Eintracht Frankfurt's ultras group, Ultras Frankfurt '97, is widely cited as one of German football's most explicitly anti-racist groups, running ongoing anti-discrimination campaigns rather than just holding a general reputation. Because those campaigns are still active, this is designated current.

Dortmund's main current ultras group, The Unity, has a publicly documented anti-fascist platform and has actually confronted far-right elements within Dortmund's own fan base - not just held an anti-fascist reputation, but acted on it. That's why the club is designated current and left-wing overall. Separately, a 1980s hooligan firm called Borussenfront was well-documented as far-right, and one of its former leaders later turned up at a far-right rally in Cologne in 2014 - but that firm is clearly a thing of the past and separate from Dortmund's current ultras scene, so it doesn't change the club's present-day rating.

Union Berlin's ultras group, Wuhlesyndikat, carries on the club's consistent anti-fascist identity, which traces back to East Berlin's working-class and quietly dissident culture under Communist rule. This is documented in mainstream German journalism, not just ultras-specific sources, and describes an ongoing identity, so this is designated current.

This one's genuinely mixed, so it's worth explaining both sides. Werder's main current ultras groups, Infamous Youth and Sherwood Youth, are described as left-leaning in German fan culture, including a documented friendship with St. Pauli's far-left ultras - that's the more visible, present-day identity, which is why the club is designated left-wing overall. But a separate hooligan crew called Endstufe-Crew Bremen was identified by anti-fascist monitors among the far-right crowd at a rally in Cologne in October 2014. Both things are true - a left-leaning ultras culture and a documented far-right hooligan crew can exist at the same club - and since the hooligan-crew incident falls within the last 20 years too, this is designated current rather than historical.

Gladbach's ultras scene, including a group called Sottocultura Ultras Gladbach, is described as generally right-leaning in German fan-forum discussions. That's a weaker source than most other entries here rely on - an informal forum rather than journalism or an official statement - but it describes an ongoing reputation, so this is designated current with lower confidence.

Cologne's fan culture, including the Wilde Horde ultras group, is generally described as anti-fascist and tolerant, in keeping with the city's broader progressive reputation in Germany. This is an ongoing reputation rather than a historical one, so it's designated current, though the sourcing is more about the general character of the fan base than a specific named campaign.

This reputation is ongoing rather than historical, hence current.

St. Pauli's anti-fascist, anti-racist identity isn't just an ultras group's opinion - it's official club policy, and the club still formally bans fascist symbolism today. Because this is a live, ongoing policy, this is designated current - it's also the strongest, most institutionally solid finding in this whole dataset.

PSG's ultras history was genuinely split for years - a far-right-linked end versus a left-wing, multicultural end - until the club disbanded all its ultras groups in 2010 after violence. The current group, Collectif Ultras Paris, formed in 2016, has organized left-leaning solidarity actions, including raising money after the 2020 Beirut port explosion. Since that's the club's present-day group acting recently, this is designated current.

Lens's ultras group, Red Tigers Lens 1994, is described as anti-racist by the same French outlet (Les Utopiques), tied to the region's coal-mining, working-class history. This is designated current as an ongoing identity.
No supporter group with a documented political position was identified for this club in the research conducted for this project.

This one deserves an honest explanation of the disagreement in the sourcing. The ultras-history site Mundo Ultras actually won't put a label on Lyon's Bad Gones, saying there's no single, stable political identity across the group's whole history. But separate news coverage is extensive and consistent over more than a decade, right up through 2026: Spanish outlets have called them 'known throughout Europe for its far-right ideology,' described their politics as nationalist and ultra-right, reported police treating them as far-right for security purposes, and covered Lyon as a known far-right hotspot tied to this group. Weighing one archive site's refusal to label them against that much ongoing, recent news coverage, this is designated current far-right - though it's a closer call than most entries here.

The French government itself - specifically the Interior Ministry, in an official circular aimed at several ultras groups - accused Strasbourg's Offenders group of being 'a neo-Nazi cell disguised as a supporters' identity.' Because this is a recent government finding, this is designated current.

This one needs some context. Toulouse's main ultras group, Indians Tolosa, is actually documented as anti-racist and officially apolitical - they display anti-racist banners and ban French flags, which carry far-right associations in French ultras culture. But a French outlet, L'Empaille, reports that this hands-off approach has let a separate, far-right group called Camside Tolosa operate within the same stand, including reportedly hosting visiting neo-Nazi hooligans in recent years. So the far-right designation here is about that specific group within Toulouse's fan base, not Indians Tolosa itself, and it's current because the tolerance of Camside Tolosa is an ongoing, recent pattern.

Reporting has tied a specific hardcore faction within Inter's fan base, called Boys San, to far-right political figures and, in some cases, neo-fascist-linked hooligan networks. This describes a historically significant faction rather than Inter's whole fan base, which doesn't carry a similar reputation as a whole - if anything, Inter's general fanbase is sometimes described as more conservative-bourgeois in a broad social sense than politically far-right in an organized way. Given the framing of Boys San as a historic faction rather than an ongoing current force, this is designated historical.

Napoli's organized support, including groups called Ultras Napoli 1972 and Fedayn EAM 1979, has a documented history of organizing against anti-Neapolitan racism - discriminatory chants that fans of northern Italian clubs have long directed at Naples. This is an ongoing form of organizing rather than something from the past, so it's designated current.

Roma's ultras group Fedayn was founded in 1972 by Roberto Rulli with openly left-wing and anarchist politics, drawn from working-class Rome neighborhoods, and set up specifically as the political opposite of Roma's other historic group, the right-wing 'Boys.' The group remains part of Roma's fan base today, which is why this is designated current even though its founding was over 50 years ago.

Juventus's ultras group, Drughi, has been tied to repeated, documented racist incidents - specifically monkey-chanting aimed at Black opposing players - across multiple recent seasons, not a single bad night years ago. Because this pattern is ongoing, it's designated current.
No supporter group with a documented political position was identified for this club in the research conducted for this project.

Atalanta's ultras group, Wild Boys (tracing back to a 1980s group called Wild Kaos), is documented as right-leaning with reported ties to the regional right-wing party Lega Lombarda. This is separate from an older, unrelated left-wing group at the same club, Brigate Nerazzurre, from the 1970s. Because Wild Boys remains an active named group today, this is designated current.

Bologna's ultras group, Curva Andrea Costa, has a documented left-wing, anti-fascist reputation, fitting with Emilia-Romagna's history as one of Italy's most left-leaning regions. This is an ongoing reputation, so it's designated current, though it rests more on general characterization than a specific campaign.

Lazio's ultras group, Irriducibili, is one of the most internationally reported far-right ultras groups in all of European football, with a long and still-ongoing history of fascist and neo-Nazi symbolism, including pro-Mussolini banners and antisemitic chants. This is probably the single most widely known case of explicit far-right ultras identity in football, and it remains an active, current reputation.

Udinese's old ultras group, Hooligans Teddy Boys, has a documented history of right-wing and racist actions, most notably a specific, widely-reported antisemitic incident in 1989 aimed at a new signing. Because the clearest documented evidence dates back more than 20 years and nothing similarly specific has been reported since, this is designated historical.

Italian political journalism (Orizzonti Politici) describes Ultras Torino as the more moderate, left-leaning faction within Torino's fan base, alongside a separate, opposing far-right splinter group in the same stand called Torino Hooligans. This describes one part of a divided fan base rather than the whole thing, and it's an ongoing, present-day split, so it's designated current.

The group is still active today, not a thing of the past, which is why this is designated current.

Ascoli's ultras group, Settembre Bianconero, is listed among the right-leaning groups in a well-known Italian newspaper mapping of ultras politics (Corriere della Sera). This is designated current since the group remains one of Ascoli's principal ultras groups today, though the sourcing is a general mapping rather than a specific incident.

Barcelona had an ultras group called Boixos Nois whose members were widely known for far-right, Catalan-separatist views. The club banned the group from the Camp Nou back in 2003 after years of violence and racism linked to them. Since then, matchday atmosphere has been led by official, club-sanctioned fan clubs with no similar reputation. Because the far-right activity dates back more than 20 years and nothing similar has replaced it since, Barcelona is designated as a club with a historical far-right sympathizer fan base rather than a current one.

Real Madrid's hardcore fan group, Ultra Sur, has a long-documented far-right reputation with ties to Francoism, and the club banned them from the Bernabeu in 2013-2014 after years of violence and racism. The group hasn't gone away, though - they've stayed in the news through an ongoing public feud with club president Florentino Perez. Because that conflict is still playing out today, Real Madrid is designated as a club with a current far-right sympathizer fan base, even though the group is officially banned from the stadium.
No supporter group with a documented political position was identified for this club in the research conducted for this project.

Atletico's Frente Atletico is one of the most written-about far-right ultras groups in European football, with a peak membership around 2,500. In 1998, members of a section of the group called 'Bastion' killed a rival Real Sociedad fan, Aitor Zabaleta, outside the old stadium - a case where an extreme political reputation led directly to a real act of violence. Frente Atletico is still active and central to Atletico's matchday support today, which is why this is designated current rather than historical.

Betis's main ultras group, Supporters Gol Sur, has been directly named as far-right-linked in Spanish investigative reporting on football's connection to far-right street movements. The group is still active and central to matchday atmosphere at the Benito Villamarin today, which is why this is designated current.

Celta's main ultras group, Celtarras, has a left-leaning, Galician-regionalist identity that's documented in Spanish football reporting. The group remains Celta's main organized support today, so this is designated current, though it's a general regional lean rather than a strongly stated political program.
No supporter group with a documented political position was identified for this club in the research conducted for this project.

Rayo's ultras group, Bukaneros, openly call themselves the 'symbol of the working class' and tie their identity to Vallecas, a historically left-wing, working-class part of Madrid. They've backed that up with real action too, including campaigning against signing a player over his reported far-right sympathies. The group has been active continuously since 1992 and remains central to Rayo's matchday support today.

Valencia's main ultras group, Ultras Yomos, has a long-running right-wing and nationalist reputation in Spanish football. A former leader of the group has also spoken publicly and identified himself as far-right, reportedly using Falangist salutes and slogans. The group remains Valencia's main ultras presence today, which is why this is designated current.

Real Sociedad's ultras group, Herri Norte Taldea, has a left-wing Basque nationalist identity tied to the Basque independence movement - a well-established, decades-old identity that's still the club's main ultras presence today.

Espanyol used to have an ultras group, Brigadas Blanquiazules, that was named as far-right in Spanish reporting on the football roots of far-right street movements. The group was officially disbanded in 2010, and matchday atmosphere today comes from two newer groups, La Curva and Juvenil 1991, that don't carry any similar political reputation. Because the far-right period ended over a decade ago and today's fan groups aren't linked to it, Espanyol is designated as a club with a historical far-right sympathizer fan base.

Athletic's ultras group, Herri Norte, shares the same left-wing Basque nationalist identity found at Real Sociedad. It's reinforced by the club itself, which only signs Basque-raised players - a policy that bakes Basque identity politics into the club, not just its fans. The group has been active since 1982 and remains Athletic's main ultras presence today.

Sevilla's ultras group, Biris Norte, has a long-running left-wing reputation and is generally considered one of the first organized ultras groups in Spanish football, founded in 1975. It's still Sevilla's main ultras group today, standing in direct contrast to city rival Real Betis's far-right-linked group.

Alaves's ultras group, Indar Gorri (Basque for 'Red Force'), shares the same left-wing Basque nationalist identity documented at Osasuna, Athletic Bilbao, and Real Sociedad. The group remains Alaves's main ultras presence today, extending this political tradition to Vitoria-Gasteiz, the Basque Country's capital.
No supporter group with a documented political position was identified for this club in the research conducted for this project.

Levante's ultras group, Frente Bukanero, has a documented left-wing, anti-fascist identity and is often described in direct contrast to city rival Valencia's right-wing Ultras Yomos. It remains Levante's main ultras group today.

Osasuna's ultras group, Indar Gorri, is described in Spanish football reporting using the specific term 'abertzale' - Basque and Navarrese left-nationalism, the same political tradition found at Athletic Bilbao, Real Sociedad, and Alaves. The group has been active since 1987 and remains Osasuna's main ultras presence today.

Racing's main ultras group, Juventudes Verdiblancas, has been active since the 1980s and, according to the Spanish ultras site Mundo Ultras, has had its public image 'associated in different periods with the far right.' The group remains Racing's central and active ultras presence today, which is why this is designated current, though the sourcing itself is a bit hedged rather than a flat, direct label.

There's some disagreement in the sourcing on Cordoba's ultras group, Brigadas Blanquiverdes: one Spanish ultras site calls them apolitical, while a more detailed fan forum - one that's checked out accurately on dozens of other groups researched for this project - says plainly that 'politically they are nationalist,' using that word the same way it describes other right-leaning Spanish groups. Given that source's track record, this leans right-wing and current, though there's no documented violence or hate speech tied to the group specifically.

Sporting Gijon's ultras group, Ultra Boys, is directly described as extreme-right in Spanish football reporting, with a documented history of physical fights that fits that reputation. The group remains active today. Worth noting: earlier research for this project had wrongly assumed this group leaned left because Asturias has a strong mining and working-class history - a good example of why guessing based on a region's general politics doesn't hold up once you actually look at the specific group.

Eibar's ultras group, Eskozia la Brava, is described in Spanish football reporting as far-left and 'abertzale' - the same Basque left-nationalist tradition documented at Athletic Bilbao, Real Sociedad, Osasuna, and Alaves. The group remains Eibar's main ultras presence today.

Cadiz's ultras group, Brigada Amarilla, is one of Spain's oldest self-identified left-wing ultras groups, with documented friendships with other left-wing Spanish groups like Rayo's Bukaneros. It remains Cadiz's main ultras presence today.
No supporter group with a documented political position was identified for this club in the research conducted for this project.

Valladolid's ultras scene actually split into two rival groups back in 1996: Ultras Violetas, described as far-right, and Fossa Garrafoni, an explicitly far-left splinter. Both groups have since been dissolved. Because that political split happened almost 30 years ago and neither group is active anymore, Valladolid is designated as a club with a historical far-right sympathizer fan base rather than a current one.

ADO Den Haag's hardcore support (North Side) has a history of racist chanting incidents, and that pattern got a lot more concrete recently: a banner from ADO's own stadium was spotted among rioters at a far-right riot in The Hague in September 2025, where people made Nazi salutes alongside Defend Netherlands, a group formally classified as far-right by Dutch intelligence. Because of that direct and very recent involvement, this is designated current.

Before the November 2024 Ajax-Maccabi Tel Aviv match, Ajax's hardcore fan group, F-Side, said 'politics and football should be kept separate' - while also saying they'd physically stop a planned pro-Palestinian protest near the stadium. Trying to shut down one side of a political demonstration is itself a political act, whatever the group says about staying neutral. This is a recent, 2024 example, which is why it's designated current.

A Portuguese sports outlet (JPN/JornalismoPortoNet) has reported on a specific far-right faction, called Divisao Azul, operating within Porto's main fan group, Super Dragoes, while noting that Super Dragoes itself doesn't publicly claim any political position. Since this describes an ongoing situation within part of the group rather than something historical, this is designated current, though it applies to a faction within Super Dragoes rather than the whole group.

Sporting's older fan group, Juventude Leonina (also called Juve Leo), used to include a specific far-right, neo-Nazi faction called Grupo 1143, led by a figure connected to Hammerskins Portugal - documented by Portuguese newspapers and its own dedicated Wikipedia article. That faction was reported inactive by 2015, and Sporting's current official fan group, Brigada Ultras Sporting, has no similar finding attached to it. Because the clearest evidence describes a now-inactive faction rather than an ongoing situation, this is designated historical.

A member of Benfica's fan group, No Name Boys, told an interviewer that swastika flags have been displayed within their section of the stadium - real hate symbolism, whatever the group claims about itself. The same member denied the group as a whole has a formal ideology, and a Portuguese police intelligence report from the mid-2000s similarly described far-right elements existing somewhere within Benfica's fan scene generally, without pinning it on one specific group. Since this describes an ongoing situation within part of the group rather than something purely historical, this is designated current, though it applies to a section of the group rather than all of it.
